lesson.menu_title: Making Proposals -- Pitching Ideas to the Board
lesson.menu_desc: Pitch an idea to your partners: rationale, scope, a pilot and a clear value proposition -- proposing structures (I would like to suggest..., Why don't we...?, What if we...?) -- 30 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 13 -- Making Proposals -- Pitching Ideas to the Board
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Maria Claudia Curimbaba | Aula 13 -- Making Proposals

listenings.text: Maria Claudia, it is Marcelo, before the partners' meeting on Thursday. I have been thinking about the idea you raised last week, and honestly, I believe you should pitch it formally to the board. Here is my read on it. The rationale is strong: our current process is slow and costs us margin, and your plan would streamline it across both US sites. So lead with that, the why, before any numbers. Keep the scope tight for now. Do not propose changing everything at once, because the board gets nervous with big rollouts. What if we framed it as a pilot first? One site, three months, a clear set of deliverables, and then we review. That is much easier to approve. On the money, be ready: they will ask for the return on the investment and the incentive for the partners. Have a simple value proposition, one sentence on what they gain. And remember, the budget for next year has not been allocated yet, so the timing is perfect. Secure buy-in from David first, he is the cautious one, and the rest will follow. You have got a viable idea here. Go and sell it.

Right, let me think this through before Thursday. I have a good idea, and for once I am not going to bury it in detail. Marcelo is right: I lead with the rationale, the why, and I keep the scope tight. So the plan is a pilot, one site, three months, with clear deliverables, and then we decide. I keep telling myself to use the strong frames, not the weak ones. Instead of saying maybe we could possibly look at something, I will say: I would like to propose that we run a pilot. Why don't we start with one site? What if we measured the results after three months? Those land much better in a boardroom. And I have to watch my old habit with the passive voice. I always want to say the proposal approved by the board, when it is the proposal was approved by the board, and the budget has been allocated, not the budget has allocated. So today the message is simple: a clear rationale, a tight scope, a low-risk pilot, and a value proposition they cannot ignore. Secure buy-in from David, then the room. Let me go and pitch it.

extra_audio.text: First, you open with the rationale: you explain why the current process is a problem and why it matters now. Then you state what you are proposing, in one clear line, and you keep the scope tight. Next, you lower the risk: instead of a full rollout, you suggest a pilot at one site, with a short timeline and clear deliverables. After that, you make the case on value: you give the return on the investment and a simple value proposition the partners cannot ignore. Finally, you ask for the decision: you secure buy-in, confirm the next step, and agree on when the results will be reviewed.
